case_name: Steed v. Rezin Orthopedics and Sports Medicine, S.C.

summary.brief: Steed v. Rezin Orthopedics and Sports Medicine, S.C.. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: On January 29, 2009, Glenn suffered a partial tear of his Achilles tendon.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On February 17, 2009, Glenn first sought treatment for his injury from Dr. Treacy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Treacy’s treatment plan included placing Glenn’s lower right leg in a plantar flexion position, with his ankle pointed in a downward direction, set in a plaster cast for six weeks.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 8, 2009, Glenn died of a pulmonary embolism.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a verdict in favor of the defendants, and the circuit court denied the plaintiff’s request for judgment notwithstanding the verdict.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Treacy testified that he would expect swelling to result from Glenn’s injury, but he acknowledged that swelling of the legs may also be considered a risk factor for DVT.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Treacy

evidence_index.text: Dr. Jimenez testified that Glenn died from a large saddle embolus, meaning that a large blood clot, shaped like a giant worm, blocked both sides of the pulmonary tree.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Jimenez

evidence_index.text: Dr. Huml, a pulmonologist, opined that, based upon a reasonable degree of medical certainty, Glenn was not at high risk for development of a DVT.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Huml

evidence_index.text: We reverse the judgment of the appellate court and affirm the judgment of the circuit court.
evidence_index.speaker: Supreme Court

evidence_index.text: Ample evidence supported a reasonable conclusion that the pulmonary embolism resulting from a propagating DVT originating from an Achilles tendon tear was not the type of injury that a reasonable medical facility would see as a 'likely result' of scheduling a follow-up appointment at three weeks, instead of two weeks.
evidence_index.speaker: Supreme Court

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: On January 29, 2009, Glenn suffered a partial tear of his Achilles tendon. He first sought treatment on February 17, 2009, reporting swelling and pain in his right ankle.

focused_exam.lower_extremity: Mild swelling of the lower leg, pain resolving

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Achilles tendon injury with risk of DVT

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with Achilles tendon injury

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Period between initial treatment and patient's death

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with Achilles tendon injury and risk factors for DVT
decision_points.prompt: What follow-up care should be scheduled?

options.description: Schedule follow-up appointment in two weeks
options.clinical_reasoning: To monitor for complications such as DVT

options.description: Schedule follow-up appointment in three weeks
options.clinical_reasoning: Delay could miss early signs of DVT

actual_action_defendant.description: Follow-up appointment scheduled for more than three weeks later

expected_action_court.description: Follow-up should have been scheduled within two weeks
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated earlier follow-up was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to schedule timely follow-up increased risk of undiagnosed DVT
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Follow-up within two weeks to monitor for DVT
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient died of a pulmonary embolism

key_findings: Jury found no breach of duty by Rezin Orthopedics

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely follow-up could have led to DVT diagnosis

factual_timeline.event: Patient suffered partial tear of Achilles tendon
Patient first sought treatment from Dr. Treacy
Patient died of a pulmonary embolism

allegations.allegation: Failure to diagnose and treat DVT

court_findings.finding: No breach of duty by Rezin Orthopedics
court_findings.legal_significance: Jury verdict in favor of defendants
